Largest Rectangle in Histogram
Stack-Based Algorithm Concept

📊 The Problem

Given an array of bar heights, find the largest rectangular area that can be formed.

Example: heights = [2, 4, 3, 2, 4]

Goal: Find the maximum area rectangle that fits within the histogram.

💡 Key Insight

Instead of checking all possible rectangles (O(n²) or O(n³)), use a monotonic stack to keep track of potential rectangles.

The Stack Metaphor:
Think of bars as customers in a "waiting room." Each bar waits for a shorter bar to arrive. When a shorter bar comes, the waiting customers (taller bars) can't extend further—we calculate their final area.

Why this works:

  • Stack maintains increasing heights
  • When we hit a shorter bar, we know how far back the taller bars can extend
  • Time complexity: O(n) instead of O(n²)

🔧 How the Algorithm Works

1 Maintain a Stack

Store pairs of (index, height) for bars with increasing heights. This forms a "waiting line" of bars that haven't encountered a blocker yet.

2 Process Each Bar

For each new bar, if it's shorter than the bar on top of the stack, we've found a "blocker." The stacked bars can't extend further right.

3 Calculate Areas

When popping a bar from the stack, we know:

  • Height: The popped bar's height
  • Right boundary: The current bar's index
  • Left boundary: Where the popped bar originally started (stored in stack)
4 Cleanup

After processing all bars, any remaining bars in the stack were never blocked. They can extend all the way to the right edge. Calculate their areas with the right boundary = array length.

🎯 The Stack Trick

When we pop a bar: We don't just know its height—we know exactly how far it can extend!

Stack: [(idx=0, h=2), (idx=1, h=4)]
Current bar: height=3 at index=2

Calculation for popped bar (idx=1, h=4):

• Right boundary: current index = 2
• Left boundary: top of remaining stack = 0
• Width: 2 - 0 - 1 = 1
• Area: 4 × 1 = 4

The "start" variable tracks where a bar could have started extending left from previous pops.

⚡ Why It's Optimal

❌ Brute Force
Check every possible rectangle combination

Time: O(n²) or O(n³)
Space: O(1)
✅ Stack Approach
Each bar processed once, pushed/popped once

Time: O(n)
Space: O(n)

The stack approach is optimal because it processes each bar exactly twice (push + pop), making it linear time.

📖 Algorithm Explanation in Plain English

The algorithm works like a queue system where bars are waiting to be "finalized." We maintain a stack of bars in increasing height order. As we process each new bar:

Step 1: Compare Current Bar to Stack Top

Look at the bar at the top of the stack. Compare its height to the current bar's height.

Two possible scenarios:
• Increasing: Current bar is taller or equal
• Decreasing: Current bar is shorter
Step 2a: If Heights are Increasing → Keep Adding

If the current bar is taller than or equal to the bar at the top of the stack, simply add the current bar to the stack. The stack remains in increasing order.

Example:
Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 4)]
New bar: height = 5
Action: Push (2, 5) → Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 4), (2, 5)]
Step 2b: If Heights are Decreasing → Start Popping

If the current bar is shorter than the bar at the top of the stack, we've found a "blocker"—the stacked bars cannot extend any further to the right. We must pop them and calculate their final rectangular areas.

What we pop and calculate:
• Popped bar's height: Use this as the rectangle's height
• Right boundary: Current bar's index (the blocker)
• Left boundary: Where the popped bar originally started
• Width: right - left = current_index - popped_index
• Area: height × width
Step 3: Keep Popping Until No More Shorter Bars

The while loop continues popping as long as:

  • The stack is not empty AND
  • The top of the stack is taller than the current bar
Example:
Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 5), (2, 6)]
New bar: height = 3

Pop iteration 1: Pop (2, 6) → Area = 6 × (3 - 2) = 6
Pop iteration 2: Pop (1, 5) → Area = 5 × (3 - 1) = 10
Stop: Top is (0, 2) which is ≤ 3, so stop popping
Step 4: Push Current Bar (With Inheritance)

After popping, the start variable has been updated to where a popped bar originally started. The current bar "inherits" this position and can extend further left than its own index.

Why this matters:
A shorter bar (height 3) can now extend back to where a taller bar (height 5) started.
This is the optimization that makes the algorithm O(n)!
🔑 The Confusing Part: Position Inheritance (`start = popped_index`)

This is the magic line that makes the algorithm O(n). Let's break it down with a real example.

Scenario:

heights = [2, 5, 6, 2, ...]

Stack so far: [(0, 2), (1, 5), (2, 6)]
Current bar: height = 2 at index = 3

Iteration 1: Pop (2, 6)

Pop (2, 6):
• Popped height = 6
• Right boundary = current index = 3
• Left boundary = top of remaining stack = index 1
• Width = 3 - 2 = 1
• Area = 6 × 1 = 6

NOW: start = 2 (popped_index)

Iteration 2: Pop (1, 5)

Pop (1, 5):
• Popped height = 5
• Right boundary = current index = 3
• Left boundary = WHERE IT STARTED = index 0 (from remaining stack)
• Width = 3 - 1 = 2
• Area = 5 × 2 = 10

NOW: start = 1 (popped_index)

Iteration 3: Stop (2 ≤ 2, no more popping)

Push the current bar with inherited position:

Push (start=1, height=2)

Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 2)]

Notice: The new bar starts at index 1, not 3!

Why This Matters:

The bar with height 2 at index 3 "inherits" the ability to extend back to index 1 because we already calculated that taller bars (5 and 6) couldn't extend past index 1 to the right. So this shorter bar can safely claim that space!

The Optimization:

Without this inheritance, we'd need to re-check how far back each bar can extend—that would be O(n²).

With inheritance: Each bar is processed once. When we pop, the next bar immediately "knows" it can extend to where the popped bar started. This is how we achieve O(n)!

Visual Analogy:

Imagine people of different heights standing in a line. When a shorter person arrives, all the taller people behind them know they "can't extend further." The shorter person then claims the space of the last person they bumped off. This is inheritance—claiming the starting position of whoever you "displaced."

Step 5: Handle Remaining Bars (Cleanup)

After processing all input bars, the stack may still contain bars that were never blocked on the right side. These bars can extend all the way to the end of the array.

Cleanup process:
For each remaining bar in the stack:
• Height: Use the bar's height
• Right boundary: len(heights) - extends to the end
• Width: len(heights) - bar's_index
• Area: height × width

Example:
Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 3)]
Array length: 5

Pop (1, 3): Area = 3 × (5 - 1) = 12
Pop (0, 2): Area = 2 × (5 - 0) = 10
💡 The Genius Move:
By keeping a monotonic increasing stack and using the start variable for position inheritance, we avoid rechecking bars. Each bar is pushed once and popped once = O(n) time!

📝 Annotated Code Logic

def largestRectangleArea_optimized(heights): max_area = 0 stack = [] # Store (starting_index, height) pairs for index, height in enumerate(heights): start = index # We hit a shorter bar! Pop waiting rectangles. while stack and stack[-1][1] > height: popped_index, popped_height = stack.pop() # Calculate area with popped bar's height width = index - popped_index area = popped_height * width max_area = max(max_area, area) # New bar inherits popped bar's starting position start = popped_index # Push current bar (may start earlier due to inheritance) stack.append((start, height)) # Cleanup: bars never blocked on the right extend to end for index, height in stack: width = len(heights) - index area = height * width max_area = max(max_area, area) return max_area
Magic Line: start = popped_index
This lets shorter bars "inherit" the starting position of taller bars. A bar of height 3 can extend as far left as the original position of a previously-popped height-5 bar.

📊 Algorithm Flowchart

Visual representation of the algorithm's decision flow and control logic:

Start/End
Decision
Process
Calculation

🔍 Example Walkthrough

Input: heights = [2, 4, 3, 2, 4]

Index 0, height=2:
Stack is empty → Push (0, 2)
Stack: [(0, 2)]
Index 1, height=4:
4 > 2 → No popping → Push (1, 4)
Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 4)]
Index 2, height=3:
3 < 4 → Pop (1, 4): Area = 4 × (2 - 1) = 4
3 > 2 → Stop popping
Push (1, 3) [bar-2 inherits bar-1's start]
Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 3)]
Index 3, height=2:
2 < 3 → Pop (1, 3): Area = 3 × (3 - 1) = 6
2 = 2 → Stop popping
Push (1, 2) [bar-3 inherits start=1]
Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 2)]
Index 4, height=4:
4 > 2 → No popping → Push (4, 4)
Stack: [(0, 2), (1, 2), (4, 4)]
Cleanup (end of array):
Pop (4, 4): Area = 4 × (5 - 4) = 4
Pop (1, 2): Area = 2 × (5 - 1) = 8 ← Maximum!
Pop (0, 2): Area = 2 × (5 - 0) = 10 ← Actually maximum!

Answer: 10 (height-2 bar extends across entire width)
